video_title: How 'Decentralization Theater' in DeFi Is Making Your Funds Less Safe
transcript: And I think a lot of applications in in DeFi engage in uh what I would describe as decentralization theater meaning um the team community whatever the the members of the multisig always retain the ability to upgrade the contracts. Uh that's true of pretty much every protocol maybe with the exception of uh of unis swap and maybe morpho um to to some degree. Um and so u that that option is always there. Um the option to uh to pause contracts in one way or another is generally uh there. Um but teams put uh barriers in the way such that their ability to use these uh uh these tools to protect users are hampered. And so you get something where there's really not this extreme degree of decentralization that you would expect or that you have let's say an Ethereum or Bitcoin where objectively it's not physically possible to force upgrade the uh the network. Um um but at the same time you don't get that benefit but at the same time you also don't get the ability to protect users in case something uh goes wrong. And so one of the decisions that we we have made at at EtherFI is uh to stop playing this game, to stop playing the sort of decentralization uh theater and introduce things like uh like blacklists, introduce things like the ability to pause contracts in an emergency uh uh situation. Um uh and that in turn it doesn't give any attacker the ability to cause harm but it does give the ability to uh the the team and the you know the systems that are monitoring the the protocol to prevent harm from uh from taking place. Um um uh and so that's something I think that I think protocols in DeFi ought to uh uh ought to embrace more broadly. And I actually think that that is probably the only way that ultimately DeFi becomes as uh safe as as trady because it's frankly not that hard to detect when you know a billion dollars of your tokens have been minted or moved. Like it's not that hard to have monitoring in place, you know, for transactions that enter the uh the memp pool that clearly violates some, you know, some crazy variant um and have the ability to immediately lock things down and prevent an attacker from uh uh you know, draining a liquidity pool or or doing something else that's uh that's dangerous. And I think that is the responsibility of every protocol to uh uh to do that to safeguard their their users assets. You can't just throw up your hands and say ah decentralization. Well, it's like well you have the ability to upgrade the contract. So u you can retain all the non-custodialness and benefits of a uh you know of of decentralization as in so far as it's appropriate for a DeFi protocol while ensuring that you're there to protect your users. And that's something that Ether is is going to be uh uh is going to continue uh to focus on. And before we move on to specific risks, I did want to ask for Isaac's response to that. Do you agree that like, you know, an app having those capabilities is only protective and not a risk in itself? >> So, I think that there's a right way of setting it up and I actually don't think that it's necessarily um against the principles of decentralization to have certain privileged sigs that can do things to protect users. Um there's ways of setting it up properly. One thing is uh like you shouldn't just have one multi-IG with no time locks that can instantly upgrade your contracts and change the behavior. That's not just a decentralization issue. That's a user safety issue. So I think that like typically a protocol should have unless it's they go the the route of having completely immutable unchangeable contracts. They should have at least three different multisigs which are delegated to three parties and it should be clear what their delegations are. one is a very fast emergency pre uh freeze uh multisig that can with a relatively low threshold act quickly. Um it can also be one that has protections in place from like a higher level multisig to prevent griefing like taking that privilege away from it but you should at least have one very low threshold emergency pause multiig that that you can distribute the rights to various people that are going to be guardians of your protocol. >> I'm sorry is that you said three signers. So is that like three of five or just three of three or like what what >> No, I mean three different multisigs altogether. And so that one could have like that one could have like it could be a one of three, it could be a two of three, it could be whatever. It could be um in um other protocols there's certain there's certain architectures where you give like one-time pause rights to a bunch of addresses and then if they use it they can't use it again um just to prevent. So you need to have an emergency pause multisig. You need to a second multisig you have is typically a D5 protocol has some you know relatively quicker updates that need to happen for parameter adjustments risk ratings and stuff that should all that should go through at least a brief time lock though there should never be a case where you need to make an immediate uh parameter update that h that takes place and can immediately be used in the same block. If we think about like adding a new type of asset to a CL a new type of collateral asset to a lending market, you should never there's never a case where you should be able to add a new type of collateral asset and deposit that asset and borrow against that asset in the same block. It's not ever needed. So that should always at least be behind like a one or two hour delay, if not like a multi-day delay. And then a third type of multisig is in charge of actually upgrading your contracts and that should be behind something that's like a two week delay. That gives your users time to leave your protocol if they don't like the direction that it's happening. You should never need to upgrade your protocol immediately. If something really bad happened, you pause and then you upgrade. Like if teams are trying to upgrade quickly like to because they don't want you to know that there's an issue, um that's a problem. But like uh to stop the bleeding you have a fast freeze. To update parameters you have a short time lock. To upgrade your contracts you have a long time lock. And those should all be spelled out in a way where it's like hey users like you have rights to leave this ecosystem. Um like I think Lido just introduced dual governance last year which basically makes it so that um like you know wrap stake eth holders can or like stake eth holders have certain governance rights to like that doesn't make them less decentralized. It's like if we're about to do a big upgrade, we're going to give you a two week uh heads up and you can leave before that if if you want to get out of there. So that's like not anti- decentralization. That's just like responsible design. >> Yeah. >> Yeah. I 100% agree with the with everything that Isaac is is saying. Um um like and I guess I I'll maybe reemphasize the point that a lot of protocols choose not to do these things in the name of decentralization. uh and my is this is like performative basically it doesn't actually uh improve the decentralization of their protocol because ultimately they do retain the ability to upgrade uh the uh the contracts um whether or not they have good protections in place against that but it does make their users unsafe. I think not having a hard big red button pause function is irresponsible. It's become kind of like a very uh like poorly defined term. like in some ecosystems the security council is in char is like these people that are you know doxed and voted on and like and and like accepted by the community to be able to take certain actions like you know reviewing a contract upgrade. What I'm a bit concerned about though is like some protocols that are trying to put like subjective uh like subjective decision-m onto these security councils around things like blacklisting and freezing. I think that is I know that you you've probably talked about this after the whole like arbitrum thing already. And it's like that's not a security council that I want you to add me to. It's like a security council should have very specific rules around like you're just making sure that we're not introducing bugs or doing things wrong. Not like so I think that there's still like questionable like does the protocol want to take on the responsibility of that blacklisting role? Like do you cuz basically you know we're sort of in this arms race where AI is just going to get better and better. So uh so I actually think the defenders are at a significant advantage. Um which is why you know generally the path that they choose is these sort of social engineering uh uh tricks where they send you a fake Zoom invite or or whatever uh in order to get access to people's machines and they're generally going after quite uh lowhanging fruit. Yeah, I'd agree that they're targeting the longtail um of of like smaller TVL protocols or maybe like devs that have like a figuring out a dev who has like a a key to do an upgrade, but like the the types of hacks that are happening there, you know, notwithstanding like the the the really uh big ones that we saw in April, like that I think those are happening a lot to a lot of like smaller smaller protocols. Um but I think that like a lot of the large protocols are wising up to it. I know that a lot of protocols are, you know, trying to bring more security work inhouse rather than just like the old days of just relying on your um auditing firm. Like they're realizing, okay, we are, even though we're a DeFi protocol, we need a CISO and we need somebody that's like making sure that like we have very robust practices for um using all these AI tools for our defense. Um, I'm more encouraged by that I think than I am scared of the attackers having access to those tools.
